result:
:::writing
There’s a moment just before a storm where the world seems to hold its breath—not in fear, but in anticipation, like a story pausing mid-sentence. The trees lean slightly, as if listening for something only they can hear. The sky, heavy and undecided, hangs low enough that you feel you could reach up and smudge it with your fingers.

I used to think that moment was empty. A gap. A delay before the real event. But lately I’ve started to suspect it might be the most important part—the inhale before something changes.

People are like that too, I think. Not the loud, obvious parts where everything is happening and everyone is watching, but the quiet edges. The pauses. The seconds where nothing seems to move, yet everything is quietly rearranging itself underneath. We rarely notice those moments in others, and almost never in ourselves.

There was a time when I measured my life in milestones. Achievements, failures, beginnings, endings. Things that could be named and pointed to. “That’s when it happened,” I would say, as if life were a series of doors I had either walked through or missed entirely.

But now I’m less sure.

Now I think most of life happens in the spaces where nothing announces itself. A random Tuesday afternoon when you realize you don’t feel the same about something anymore. A conversation you barely remember that quietly alters how you see the world. The slow drift of becoming someone else, so gradual you only notice it when you try to return to who you were and find that person doesn’t quite fit anymore.

It’s unsettling, in a way. There’s no clear map for these changes, no marker that says “you are here.” Just a vague sense that something has shifted, like furniture moved in the dark.

I wonder how many versions of ourselves we’ve already left behind without ceremony. How many identities have quietly expired while we were busy looking ahead, or back, or anywhere but at the present moment where the change was actually happening.

And what about the things we carry forward without realizing? The habits, the fears, the small rituals that feel like personality but might just be echoes. Maybe we’re less consistent than we like to believe. Maybe we’re constantly rewriting ourselves, just slowly enough to pretend we’re stable.

There’s something comforting about that, though. If we’re always in motion, then nothing about us is truly fixed. Not the parts we’re proud of, and not the parts we wish we could outgrow. Everything is negotiable, given enough time and enough unnoticed Tuesdays.

The storm, when it finally comes, is never quite what you expect. It’s louder, or quieter. Shorter, or longer. Sometimes it misses you entirely and you’re left standing in a world that looks unchanged, even though you were ready for something dramatic.

But the air afterward—that’s what gives it away. It feels different. Cleaner, maybe. Or just rearranged in a way you can’t quite describe. The world hasn’t transformed into something new, but something subtle has shifted, and you can feel it even if you can’t prove it.

I think that’s how change works most of the time. Not as a spectacle, but as a quiet adjustment to the atmosphere of your life.

You wake up one day and realize that something that used to matter deeply now feels distant. Or that something you never paid attention to has become essential. There’s no single moment you can point to and say, “That’s when it happened.” It just… did.

And maybe that’s okay.

Maybe we don’t need every transformation to be dramatic or visible. Maybe it’s enough that they happen at all. That we keep shifting, keep adapting, keep becoming—even if we don’t always notice it in real time.

The world doesn’t announce its changes with certainty. It hints. It nudges. It offers small signs that something is different, if you’re willing to pay attention.

A tree bending a little further than it used to. A silence that feels fuller instead of empty. A thought that lingers longer than it should.

And you, standing somewhere in the middle of it all, not quite who you were, not yet who you’re becoming, but undeniably in motion.

Like the air before a storm, holding everything it needs, waiting for the moment it lets go.
:::
